claims.claim: The SEI layer breaks down at a temperature between 80 and 120°C, releasing reducing gases including H2 and C2H4.
claims.quote: SEI layer breaks down at a temperature between 80 and 120 ◦C in the battery, releasing reducing gases including H2 and C2H4.

claims.claim: For NCM batteries, when the internal temperature exceeds 220°C, the redox reaction between cathode and anode occurs, which can instantly raise the internal temperature to above 900°C.
claims.quote: For Li(NixCoyMn1-x-y)O2 (NCM) battery, when the internal temperature of the battery exceeds 220 ◦C, the redox reaction will occur between the cathode and the anode material, and the reaction will be carried out simultaneously with a large number of side reactions, which can instantly raise the internal temperature of the battery to >900 ◦C.

claims.claim: Reducing gas generated at the anode-electrolyte interface migrates to the cathode, reacts with cathode material, causes early degradation, releases heat, and advances the decomposition reaction temperature of the cathode.
claims.quote: the reducing gas generated at the anode - electrolyte interface freely migrates to the highly oxidized cathode, which then reacts with the cathode material, causing an early degradation of the cathode material and leading to a large amount of heat release. Wang et al. [51] revealed the effect of reducing gas attack in the early stages of TR on heat accumulation and TR triggering, and found that reducing gas can further advance the decomposition reaction temperature of the cathode.

claims.claim: The solid-liquid phase change reaction of the cathode material starts at 140°C, generating CO2 and releasing O2 in a three-phase solid-liquid-gas mixture reaction.
claims.quote: the solid-liquid phase change reaction of the cathode material starts at a temperature of 140 ◦C. This process was tested to the generation of CO2, which is involved in the reaction between the cathode and the electrolyte, at which time it is a three-phase solid-liquid-gas mixture reaction and releases O2.

claims.claim: CO2, CO, H2, C2H4, CH4, and C2H6 make up more than 90% of the total vent gases from LIB thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Among them, CO2, CO, H2, C2H4, CH4 and C2H6 make up the majority of the gases that are released, accounting for >90 % of the total vent gases.

claims.claim: At 0.2 MPa external pressure, thermal runaway propagation speed increases by approximately 65% compared to 0 MPa.
claims.quote: external pressure accelerates thermal runaway propagation (TRP), with propagation speed at 0.2 MPa increasing by approximately 65 % compared to 0 MPa.

claims.claim: Total gas yield decreases by approximately 28% at 0.1 MPa and 53% at 0.2 MPa relative to 0 MPa external pressure.
claims.quote: gas evolution analysis reveals a substantial reduction in total gas yield with increasing external pressure, exhibiting decreases of about 28 % at 0.1 MPa and 53 % at 0.2 MPa relative to 0 MPa.

claims.claim: External pressures of 0.1 and 0.2 MPa enhance interfacial contact within the battery, thereby increasing internal thermal conductivity and resulting in a more uniform temperature distribution, which raises the TR initiation temperature.
claims.quote: external pressures of 0.1 and 0.2 MPa enhance interfacial contact within the battery, thereby increasing internal thermal conductivity. This improvement results in a more uniform temperature distribution, which raises the TR initiation temperature

claims.claim: Increasing external pressure shifts the initial TR location inward from the battery edge toward the center.
claims.quote: shifts the initial TR location inward from the battery edge toward the center

claims.claim: The reduction in total gas yield with increasing external pressure is primarily attributed to earlier safety venting and prolonged electrolyte evaporation periods.
claims.quote: This reduction is primarily attributed to earlier safety venting and prolonged electrolyte evaporation periods.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, thermal runaway was observed during nail penetration, attributed to the occurrence of a secondary ISC, whereas batteries tested at lower SOC levels (0% and 50% SOC) did not show fire or explosion under similar experimental conditions.
claims.quote: At 100% SOC, thermal runaway was observed, attributed to the occurrence of a secondary ISC. By contrast, batteries tested at lower SOC levels (0% and 50% SOC) did not show fire or explosion, despite being subjected to similar experimental conditions.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway propagation in LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1-based batteries is eightfold faster than in LiFePO4-based batteries.
claims.quote: cathode chemistry (thermal runaway propagation of LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1‐based batteries is eightfold faster than that of LiFePO4‐based batteries)

claims.claim: When temperatures exceeded 233°C, all batteries initiated thermal runaway, triggered by significant shrinkage of the separator and reactions related to the cathode.
claims.quote: When temperatures exceeded 233°C, all batteries initiated thermal runaway, triggered by significant shrinkage of the separator and reactions related to the cathode.

claims.claim: Under high SOC conditions greater than 75% SOC, nails with higher conductivity enhanced internal Joule heat release, leading to more intense thermal runaway behavior, whereas under low SOC conditions less than 50% SOC, nails with higher conductivity helped dissipate heat, weakening the intensity of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Under high SOC conditions (greater than 75% SOC), nails with higher conductivity enhanced the internal Joule heat release of the battery, leading to more intense thermal runaway behavior. In contrast, under low SOC conditions (less than 50% SOC), nails with higher conductivity helped dissipate heat, thus weakening the intensity of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: At 90% SOC during nail penetration simulation, the battery temperature could reach up to 800°C with a voltage drop of 1.9 V.
claims.quote: At 90% SOC, the temperature could reach up to 800°C, with a voltage drop of 1.9 V.

claims.claim: Ceramic nails were more likely to trigger higher-temperature thermal runaway flames and increased fire risk compared to steel nails, and increased penetration depth and diameter led to greater mass loss with ceramic nails causing more damage than steel nails.
claims.quote: Ceramic nails were more likely to trigger higher‐temperature thermal runaway flames, increasing the fire risk compared to steel nails. Increased penetration depth and diameter led to greater mass loss, with ceramic nails causing more damage than steel nails.

claims.claim: The contact resistance for a nail in contact with pure active material film is approximately three orders of magnitude greater than that of the nail-to-bare-metal-foil configuration.
claims.quote: the combination of a nail with a pure active material film yields the highest contact resistance, which is approximately three orders of magnitude greater than that of the nail/bare metal foil setup.

claims.claim: Increasing nail penetration speed leads to a shorter time to thermal runaway and a higher peak temperature, and temperature nonuniformity within the battery intensifies at higher penetration speeds.
claims.quote: an increase in penetration speed leads to a shorter time to thermal runaway and a higher peak temperature. Mao et al. [33] observed that as the nail penetration speed increases, temperature nonuniformity within the battery intensifies, indicating that high‐speed penetration causes more severe damage.

claims.claim: Cells at 100% SOC retained approximately 53.7% of their initial mass after nail penetration thermal runaway, compared to 96.7% at 20% SOC, indicating that cells at higher SOC expel more battery components during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: cells retained approximately 53.7% of their initial mass at 100% SOC, as opposed to 96.7% at 20% SOC, indicating that cells at higher SOC expel more battery components during thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Solid-state architectures showed 0% thermal runaway incidence in nail penetration tests.
claims.quote: solid‐state architectures showing 0% thermal runaway incidence in nail penetration tests
